confidence_rationale: This paper provides the most fully developed precision-weighting account of psychosis within the active inference framework. It treats all positive psychotic symptoms -- hallucinosis, delusions, sensory attenuation deficits, catatonia -- as expressions of a single core pathology: aberrant encoding of precision (NMDA receptor dysfunction modulating the gain of prediction error units). This maps directly onto MECH-244's claim that psychosis is a precision-weighting failure where top-down priors persistently dominate. Confidence 0.80 because the theoretical framework is highly developed and consistent with neurobiological data, though direct experimental tests of the precision-weighting account specifically (vs competing models) remain limited.
failure_signatures: The paper is primarily theoretical; direct empirical tests of the precision-weighting account in human psychosis samples are not provided within the paper itself.

Hallucination and delusion are treated as unified expressions of the same precision pathology; MECH-245 (hallucination) argues they are mechanistically distinct -- this paper does not fully support that dissociation.
The NMDA receptor substrate for precision encoding is mechanistically coherent but not yet definitively established as the primary pathology in all psychosis presentations.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Psychotic symptoms -- hallucinations, delusions, abnormal eye movements, sensory attenuation deficits, catatonia -- are all expressions of a single core pathology: aberrant encoding of precision (the confidence or inverse uncertainty associated with beliefs at different levels of the cortical hierarchy). Precision is encoded by the post-synaptic gain of prediction error units, regulated by NMDA receptors and dopaminergic neuromodulation. When precision is aberrantly allocated -- too high to prior beliefs, too low to sensory prediction errors -- the generative model becomes self-sealing: top-down predictions dominate bottom-up sensory evidence, producing percepts that are not grounded in current sensory input (hallucinosis) and beliefs that are not updated by contrary evidence (delusions).
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-244 states that psychosis is a precision-weighting failure where prior beliefs persistently dominate sensory evidence; the generative model becomes self-sealing and sensory update is suppressed. Adams et al. 2013 is the canonical formal articulation of this claim in the active inference framework. The REE translation is: in the E1/E2 architecture, the bottom-up prediction error signal (sensory mismatch driving E1 updates) is discounted relative to top-down predictions (the prior generative model). The world model encoded in E1/E2 cannot be updated by experience because precision is aberrantly concentrated at the prior level. This is mechanistically distinct from confabulation (MECH-094, which is a write-gate failure allowing simulated content to enter phi(z)) and from hallucination (MECH-245, which is generative dominance producing false percepts); psychosis involves the world model itself becoming unfalsifiable by incoming sensory evidence.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Adams et al. treat hallucination and delusion as co-expressed expressions of the same precision pathology; the REE framework requires these to be mechanistically distinct (MECH-244 vs MECH-245). The paper's unified account does not fully support the dissociation required by REE's three-way taxonomy. Additionally, the precision-weighting account does not specify which component of the REE architecture (E1 encoder, E2 transition model, or E3 evaluation) is most directly disrupted; the translation to the specific E1/E2 error signal framing requires additional inference.
mapping.source_context: Theoretical review by Adams (UCL/Cambridge), Stephan (ETH Zurich), Frith and Friston (UCL). Published in Frontiers in Psychiatry 2013 as part of the active inference programme. Over 760 citations; represents the current orthodoxy in computational psychiatry accounts of psychosis.

confidence_components.notes: Exceptional source quality: high-impact paper from the founders of the active inference framework. Mapping fidelity good: precision-weighting failure maps directly to MECH-244 but the unified treatment of hallucination and delusion slightly complicates the dissociation from MECH-245 required by REE. Transfer risk low: the framework is developed for human psychosis specifically.
